item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced and is thus assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What spelling and grammar? Like 30 people have said this and never told me what the fuck they're talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: other than the spelling and grammar errors I don't know why this is rated so low. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was mostly just confused by this, especially the large amounts of medical terminology. I'm afraid I don't know too much about classes of haemorrhage. I had to read it a few times to get what it did (it cures you… to an extent). The bit where the man's TB was cured but his HIV was unaffected could really use some clarifying.

And then even when I understood it all, I still found it a little underwhelming. Never been a huge fan of things that do what they're supposed to do, despite what they're supposed to do having been debunked by science.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is under the control of EchoFourDelta.

Mine again and stuff cause reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, he got rid of the TB, hence it saying he still has HIV/AIDS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When he visited his primary physician again, he was found not even to have a latent infection. The treatments did not, however, cure his HIV/AIDS.

Wait, it got rid of the HIV infection, but he still had AIDS?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You British and your… um… letter… stuff…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where are the spelling mistakes? I caught a number of them when I did my own read-through, but I see none now.

EDIT: Ninja'ed by Flames.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: KrackoCloud, I didn't see any mistakes, either, although there are a couple of instances of Commonwealth spellings rather than American. Are those what you're thinking of?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you point them out? Neither me or my spell checker is catching anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're looking for the missing parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Not feeling it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The basic concept is pretty neat. Something about the addendum throws me off, though. I guess it sounds just a bit too much like a story, and not enough like a report. Also there are a few spelling mistakes here and there. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the contrary, I thought the disappear-o girlfriend was interesting enough to save it from a downvote. I'm hoping for more on the kit, with some exposition on her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ms M probably had things explained in a fairly vague manner. Lying and making some bogus story as to why you're asking all these questions isn't always going to get you the best results, and if they're just going to either keep her, or give her an amnestic, why not take the approach that's best for getting the info they want?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh, this seems like another "object that works as creators intended." The interview didn't add anything, and I'm a bit curious how Dr. M was able to ascertain the function of the Foundation ("Don't you have like, I don't know, ghosts or sasquatches to catch?"). The object itself is uninteresting, and since the girlfriend just vanishes without leaving any sort of clue, my only reaction to the article was "Yeah, and…?" Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, dormant removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he means redundant. Latent and dormant are more or less the same thing (although I'd use 'latent').

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Latent, dormant TBC infection, for Bast's sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contradictory? I'm not sure I understand how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When he visited his primary physician again, he was found not even to have a latent, dormant infection. The treatments did not, however, cure his HIV/AIDS.

Erm, what? This seems mildly contradictory.

The interview adds almost nothing to the article, beyond implying the existence of something for the other humours, and is too long to support just that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll work on that, and yeah italics should do it. Thanks :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the scarificator is used on a subject with an illness or disease, or one similar to one found in the item's time period, the subject is cured.

^This sentence needs to be restructured to be more clear.

The non-speech stuff in the interview log ought to be in some way set apart from the speech parts. Italics, maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a number of typos. Unsure what I think of the article overall. Thinkin' on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels a bit like an OTT item to me. That being said, the item itself is unique enough that I'll give it some leeway just because lots of people have probably never heard of it before. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good points on most accounts. As for the medical emergency, it was the fact that the bleed from a cut less than a centimetre deep could not be stopped. I don't think they were counting on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not digging it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Fixed what I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Let's take a look
- Hide commentary

Employees may submit a request for treatments at the referral of a site physician.

I will never upvote an article that uses the skip as the employee health plan. This never, ever, under any circumstances, makes sense.

SCP-1392 is an antique scarificator, dating back to approximately 1883. The components of the object consist of brass, with the exceptions of an ebony handle and blades of surgical steel.

This is awkwardly phrased. I feel like "originally manufactured in 1883" works better than "dating back". You could also just say "the object consists of brass components, with an ebony handle etc.", since "with the exception" implies that it's almost entirely brass, which doesn't appear to be true.

The brass and ebony components are in outstanding repair

"outstanding repair" sounds unscientific.

Like mundane scarificators

"Mundane" is bad tone. "Like scarificators of similar manufacture" would work better.

When the scarificator is used on a subject with an illness or disease similar to on found in the item's time period

An illness or disease similar to one found in 1883? So not the actual illnesses, then. Also, does this only apply to illnesses discovered by the late 19th century? Those documented by western medicine? And then there's the fact that we're mostly dealing with the same diseases now as then; TB, measles, syphilis, influenza, etc., these are all the same things.

Addendum 1392-01:

I don't get how this was supposed to work. It sounds like the Foundation had a nurse conducting an experiment on D-Class with the intended effect of inducing unconsciousness, but then when it happens it's like this big medical emergency, to the point that she has to call a physician. Why the hell is a physician not around in the first place when they're carrying out medical experiments using anomalous artifacts?

The hope was to find the upper limits of the objects effect

Poor tone.

Unfortunately, the lack of blood flow to the brain over the extended period cause irreversible brain damage, and he was terminated

Use of "unfortunately" here is poor tone. Also, "subject" instead of "he" would work a lot better.

he visited a local naturopath for a more natural treatment

The lack of any qualifiers or quotation marks implies that the Foundation believes that naturopaths do in fact provide a "more natural treatment", whatever that means.

Ms. M: Fucking finally. This seems like a lot of work for a stupid trinket. Don't you have like, I don't know, ghosts or sasquatches to catch?

Man, we really need to stop telling people who we are and what we do when we take them into custody.

Ms. M: She never said anything else about them, but I assumed they affected the other three humours.

Granted, I don't use naturopaths as I believe in actual science and medicine, but I don't think they subscribe to humorism.

This could have done with a proofreading, and it feels way too familiar and subjective for a supposedly clinical document. The concept doesn't do a whole lot for me, and the mysterious "I'm going to give skips to people so they can use them for some reason, then magically disappear" person at the end is tacked-on and doesn't add anything. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It doesn't bring enough that's new to the table. Maybe if it let out one of the other humors?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this quite a bit. Also you've introduced me to a thing I didn't even know existed.

+1 for you

Grammar/spelling/flow nit-picks
Grammar/spelling/flow nit-picks

illness or disease similar to on found in

Shouldn't that be 'one'?

the lack of blood flow to the brain over the extended period cause irreversible brain damage

Shouldn't that be 'caused'?

Further testing on the negative feedback threshold found the effect to happen between a Class II and a Class III haemorrhage.

This sentence strikes me as super awkward, and I wish something could be done with it. But since I'm focusing on this stuff, you've clearly done your job already. Hope it goes over well for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehh, like I said on chat, this just doesn't do anything for me I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
illness or disease similar to on found in the item's time period

This sentance just seems off to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, especially what with the existence of the rest of the kit affecting remaining humours. I'm curious what of the one that deals with black bile, given the fluid doesn't exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Iiiiinteresting. Blood-letting that works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did a thing. Echo helped a lot. Blablah.
